speaker: interviewer
text: Rose Dora Presser, take number four. Rose, you were telling me about coming to Australia. How did you travel to Australia in 1959?

speaker: interviewee
text: We go by train to Rome. And from Rome, we go by-- not by ship. We go to the-- Italy. To-- what's the name of the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sicily.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Sicily, we traveled to Sicily. We introduce-- we have been introduced to spaghetti on the train. My son he knows always what they're eating. Mom, they have spaghetti here, first time, you know, spaghetti. [INAUDIBLE]. And we come to Sicily. I don't like very much really. The hotel was so bad. So like the rooms really not-- not very nice, nothing in it. But the ship was thousand of German, only one family, Jewish family we have been. Thousand of German come from Bremen and come through over there, and we go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the ship? What were you allowed to take out of Poland when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm. Don't remind me. We take so many things. Maybe 10-- maybe, I don't know, maybe 10 boxes like half of this room. Everything what you see this is in Poland every little piece. We-- why we take-- but they tell us, bring everything, bring everything that we bring.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left Poland did you still have family there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my brother was in [INAUDIBLE], my brother was. And my other brother was on the way to Australia, too. Yes, but that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how was the trip on the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice, very nice, we dance in the evening. It was a nice ship, was air conditioned, was very nice. The food was not nice, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Germans on board know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I was not-- not interested in them. Very, very nice-- they come to Australia like to stay in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, where did you land in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in Port Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your first impression of the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, I was shocked, because we traveled from the-- [INAUDIBLE] from the-- from the port. We say, and I say, where is the city? Where is the city? [INAUDIBLE] nothing around us. It was just-- that's it. Nice now and they finally coming, you know, this was a shock when we come, a big shock for me-- completely different life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you like Australian people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much-- they're charming. They're lovely people. For me, they have been very good. When we have milk bar, they always help us, you know, because we didn't speak English. [LAUGHS] So they saw, you know, [? patient ?] and they show you. They know more about this very something like I because we just come into this. They're very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your milk bar?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Toorak, we started. Was a beautiful milk bar, too. Today, it is one milk bar in Toorak Village. There's the milk bar that we used to have.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find the Jewish community in Melbourne?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met a lot of friends from school, even. Yes, I have friends from school. Very nice, I mean, you-- you see a few people. You know, you can't see everybody. You see only time a few people. I play cards. I see the same people. And I play Bridge and Aces, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you hear about the book

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean? My children brought me the book. My children bring me the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Mr. Keneally interview you for the book?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yes, he-- we have a picture even with him. I show you this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: After, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I show you the last time. He was here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, that's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a supper together, yes. No, but it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did do you think about the idea to write a book about Schindler's ark?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good-- he need, he-- he deserve it. He deserve this. That he was-- that-- they they remember about him. Yes, we didn't do much about this, but it's beautiful. It was remember his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you seen the movie

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you like the movie?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very good movie. I was impressed with the movie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel that it reflected your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all, not at all, not at all. But-- but for the people who never was over there it's very good. You know, you  can't make this movie same story about that or something, should be everything. Very well done-- fantastic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you tell people when they ask where you were during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say I was in concentration camp with Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they know Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, nearly everybody know him-- even the Australian people know him. I mean, from the book, from the film.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think the film makes it easier for you to talk about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you that I never talk about this. I don't know. My-- my son is even angry that I-- but he says, Mom, [INAUDIBLE]. Listen I was lucky I go-- I go around this whole things. I never saw some really tragic. I saw the chimney. I know that the people are over there and this, but I didn't see this, you know? I was very lucky. I was very lucky. We have been very lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you tell your children and your grandchildren about your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they ask me I tell them. They need this for school. So I tell them whatever I-- if they ask me the question. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think you might go back to Poland one day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't go. I was over there-- near. I was in Hungary. We go by car for one months in Europe, but I didn't go to Poland. Because my brother was already dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Maybe I should go to cemetery or something. But I don't know. We didn't want to go. We didn't go to Poland. I was everywhere, but I didn't go. I go to America. I go to here and there to California. I didn't go to Canada. I'm invited to Canada, but-- but we-- but I didn't want to go to Poland. Not especially because I-- I haven't got nothing. I can say nothing wrong. Because I was very lucky in Poland. I tell, you I never feel that I am somebody else or something. I was very lucky. I feel good in Poland. I was in [INAUDIBLE] when I come to Australia I cry. I was very unhappy for the beginning. It was completely different life for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: And now are you happy in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'm very happy. I did forgot about it. Now I'm old woman, but it's different. But the kids are happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you why you chose to do this interview today? Why you decided to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you want me to do it. [LAUGHS] I don't want to do it to tell you the truth. I was really afraid to do it, to remember everything. But you say, you should, you should. And I done it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything your experience during the war years has taught you and helped you with today?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- what did you say?

speaker: interviewer
text: What has your experience taught you? How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the experience a very bad experience. I don't think so what they can teach me-- teach me something.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: I live with the people-- always good with everybody, you know? I love people. I-- I have a coffee lunch here for 25 years. I don't know how long we have been in the business. And I love everybody who come.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I love people, you know? And I'm very happy here. I meet my customers. If they catch me, they love me too. And does matter who-- who come for cup of coffee or who come for dinner. I like to be with the people. You have a message you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Try to be happy. When the kids are happy, I'm happy. I come just for the children here. Because for me, I can stay in Poland where we were. But for children, I come here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I believe that it's a beautiful country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would you like for your children in the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that they have been healthy and happy, and my grandchildren too. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you for telling your story today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, thank you very much, too, because I would never have the courage to go to say something. Thank you very much. You're very charming-- you all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother Helena Ameisen. And this my father [? Maritza ?] Ameisen in [INAUDIBLE] might be [INAUDIBLE] in 19-- in 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you have this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] from Israel. My sister have in Israel, because we send her, you know. And she take from home, because maybe she take when she was going.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is [? Olga ?] Ameisen my cousin from my husband, from my father's side-- that cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the glasses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and this my mother Helena Ameisen. And it's me Rose Ameisen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe eight or seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the city, in-- in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [INAUDIBLE]. Her name was name was Rosa-- Rosa [? Bergler ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: With the glasses here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. This is Olek Ameisen, my brother. He live in Sydney, still alive. Thank God. And this was me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old  are you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Maybe I was here. Maybe 12 or 14, you know? [? I don't know, ?] maybe 14, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who is in this photo from left to right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. [? Reinshtein ?] my neighbor. Immediate name I don't remember. And [? Marilla ?] and [? Sumack ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the last name of [? Marilla ?] and--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Marilla ?] [? Billet ?], [? Billet-- ?] already she was [? Billet? ?] Or-- [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know if she was married at this time. Oh, sure she was. She was much older.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Summak? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Summak. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Rosenburg. ?] And me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the end here?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my brother Poldek Ameisen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the occasion of this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have some motorcycles, you know. [INAUDIBLE] Maccabi

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maccabi was some-- some-- some ride around by there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was maybe [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] '32? '32? Maybe, no, maybe later. What I am talking-- '35.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Maccabi [INAUDIBLE] Maccabi-- it was a Jewish Cracow barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, OK. Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I know it's only me, some strangers. I went to pick up children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: First time I make over there and get the children. I've never met in my life. This was first time.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in the Cracow ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in '39, '40-- maybe '40 when the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose bakery was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [INAUDIBLE]? [INAUDIBLE]? These people have been. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did-- this-- what did you say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That this is me here.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the white shirt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, how old were you here?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I see arm band under my hand. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say you're wearing an arm band?

speaker: interviewee
text: I see arm band, but you can't see because the white blouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: A friend of mine, he was in the balcony in my place. We live over there. And he took the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you have this picture today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess always in Israel or it was-- I have a box of pictures what I have in Polish people. You know and I take after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, I have more from during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Olek Ameisen. This is my brother. He live in Sydney. This me and my husband, Bernard Presser. And I have first [INAUDIBLE] knew what I had from the-- when the war started. My skirt and blouse-- that's what I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the [INAUDIBLE] factory over there in the [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: In Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cracow, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean-- day?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year? It was maybe one year after the war. One year after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945-- Five or six? Five, six, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the cigarette?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cigarette is Michael Presser and John Presser.

speaker: interviewer
text: One when were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: You tell me. I forgot this. What is the year? John is 49. Michael is 45.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Ramesh Presser, my grandson. Joshua Presser, Frances Presser-- all my grandchildren-- Danielle Presser and Ravi Presser.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: 1989-- And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: They say. I don't know, maybe. Botanical garden, no? Here--

speaker: interviewer
text: Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Australia, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They born in Australia-- all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see--

speaker: interviewer
text: Start in the back with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this my brother, son-in-law, my bother. And this my brother and then next to him Olek-- Olek Ameisen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the red top?

speaker: interviewee
text: This [? Maya, ?] my sister-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beside her?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is friend of ours. This-- this is my niece Helena. Helena.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, down in front in the stripe?

speaker: interviewee
text: This me and my husband, [INAUDIBLE], this is my-- my niece daughter. And this is the son, [? Libel ?].

speaker: interviewer

speaker: interviewee
text: In Surfers Paradise, now, this year.
